straight_beat.name: Kenji Hollingsworth
straight_beat.column:
The Mariners’ 9-6 loss to the Astros yesterday was a night of extremes. By the numbers, the offense looked like a team that had finally found its rhythm, but the bullpen’s collapse turned a potential sweep into a split. Seattle’s 9 runs were the most they’d scored in a game since April 22nd, a streak of 13 games without a 9-run performance. The numbers don’t lie: their team batting average (.258) is just below the league average, but their run production has been flirting with the top third of the AL.  

The key here isn’t just the 9 runs, though that’s a start. It’s the way they got them. The Mariners’ lineup, particularly the middle order, continued to show flashes of the kind of offensive firepower that made them a playoff contender in 2022. Kyle Seager led the charge with three RBIs, including a two-run double in the fifth inning that turned a 3-3 game into a 5-3 lead. Meanwhile, the young sluggers—J.D. Davis and Taylor Trammell—combined for four hits and three runs, their offensive contributions echoing the kind of production that made the 2022 team so dangerous. The numbers say this lineup is capable of scoring runs, and the Astros’ pitching staff, for all its dominance, couldn’t contain them.  

But then there’s the bullpen

optimist.name: Rainy Beckerman
optimist.column:
The Mariners lost yesterday, but let’s not let that cloud the fact that this team is still building something special. Sure, the Astros handed them a 6-9 defeat, but look at the tape. Look at the kids stepping up, the way the offense is starting to click, the way the bullpen’s got that fire in its belly. This isn’t just another loss—it’s a blueprint.  

J-Rod could strike out four times and I’d still come home humming. This is the team that finally, finally, feels like it belongs to us. The 2022 season broke the curse, but this? This is the encore. The front office didn’t just draft these kids—they built a culture. A culture where guys like Lewis and Rodriguez are learning to trust each other, where the rotation’s got a chance to settle, and where the offense is starting to remind us what it means to score runs.  

You can’t ignore the record, sure. Five wins, nine losses,

pessimist.name: Walt Varga
pessimist.column:
Another quiet offense, another wasted start. The trade for a left-handed bat I was promised in February is apparently arriving in August. Of 2031.  

The Mariners’ loss yesterday wasn’t just another defeat—it was a masterclass in futility, a performance so predictably mediocre it feels scripted. Houston’s 6-9 victory wasn’t just about the Astros’ resilience; it was about Seattle’s inability to do anything right. The offense, that fragile thing they’ve tried to build since the Kingdome, once again resembled a group of players who’ve never held a bat in their lives. Strikeouts, ground balls, and a lineup that looks like it’s been assembled by a sleep-deprived intern. You’d think with the payroll they’ve splurged on, there’d be some semblance of competence. Instead, we get a game where the Mariners score nine runs and still lose, because the bullpen can’t protect a lead and the defense can’t stop a .250
